title: The Tailor's Measuring Tape
text_zh: 弄堂里开了二十多年的裁缝铺即将关门，老裁缝姓陆，七十二岁，手里那把黄色的软皮尺比他的婚姻还长——用了快五十年，刻度已经模糊得看不清了，但他说用不着看，手感比刻度准。他量体裁衣的动作有一种催眠式的优雅：皮尺从肩头滑下，在腰间绕一圈，再顺着手臂蜿蜒而下，整个过程不超过三十秒，但他能从中读出客人站姿的偏斜、脊柱的弧度、甚至职业的痕迹。他说长期伏案工作的人右肩会比左肩高一两公分，经常负重的人背部肌肉分布不对称——这些身体密码只有在量体的那三十秒里才会短暂地向裁缝敞开。「我的皮尺量的不是尺寸，」他说，「是一个人身体的故事。」最让我感慨的是他的态度：他从不评判这些故事，不会说你太胖了应该减肥，也不会说你驼背了应该纠正，他只是安静地接收信息，然后剪出一件让你穿上以后看起来最自在的衣服。也许这就是手工的本质——它不试图改造人，只是温柔地包裹人此刻的样子。
text_pinyin: Nongtang li kai le ershi duo nian de caifeng pu jijiang guanmen, lao caifeng xing Lu, qishi'er sui, shou li na ba huangse de ruan pichi bi ta de hunyin hai chang—yong le kuai wushi nian, kedu yijing mohu de kan bu qing le, dan ta shuo yong bu zhao kan, shougan bi kedu zhun. Ta liangticalyi de dongzuo you yi zhong cuimian shi de youya: pichi cong jiantou hua xia, zai yao jian rao yi quan, zai shun zhe shoubi wanyan er xia, zhenggege guocheng bu chaoguo sanshi miao, dan ta neng cong zhong du chu keren zhanzhi de pianxie, jizhu de hudu, shenzhi zhiye de henji. Ta shuo changqi fuan gongzuo de ren youjian hui bi zuojian gao yi liang gongfen, jingchang fuzhong de ren beibu jirou fenbu bu duichen—zhexie shenti mima zhi you zai liangti de na sanshi miao li cai hui duanzan de xiang caifeng changkai. 'Wo de pichi liang de bu shi chicun,' ta shuo, 'shi yi ge ren shenti de gushi.' Zui rang wo gankai de shi ta de taidu: ta cong bu pingpan zhexie gushi, bu hui shuo ni tai pang le yinggai jianfei, ye bu hui shuo ni tuobei le yinggai jiuzheng, ta zhi shi anjing de jieshou xinxi, ranhou jian chu yi jian rang ni chuan shang yihou kan qilai zui zizai de yifu. Yexu zhe jiu shi shougong de benzhi—ta bu shitu gaizao ren, zhi shi wenrou de baoguo ren cike de yangzi.
text_en: The tailor's shop in the alley, open for over twenty years, is about to close. The old tailor, surnamed Lu, is seventy-two. The yellow soft measuring tape in his hand has lasted longer than his marriage—nearly fifty years, its markings too faded to read, but he says he doesn't need to look; touch is more accurate than markings. His measuring movements have a hypnotic elegance: the tape slides from the shoulder, wraps around the waist, then winds down the arm—the whole process under thirty seconds, yet from it he reads the client's postural tilt, spinal curvature, even occupational traces. He says desk workers' right shoulders are one or two centimeters higher than the left; people who regularly carry heavy loads have asymmetrical back muscles—these body codes only briefly open themselves to the tailor during those thirty seconds of measuring. 'My tape doesn't measure dimensions,' he says, 'it measures the story of a person's body.' What moves me most is his attitude: he never judges these stories—never says you're too fat and should diet, never says your posture needs correction. He quietly receives the information, then cuts a garment that makes you look most at ease when wearing it. Perhaps this is the essence of handcraft—it doesn't try to remake people, only gently wraps them as they are right now.

title: The Dog Walker's Theory
text_zh: 小区里有一位职业遛狗师，她叫阿秋，每天同时遛六七只不同人家的狗。我经常在傍晚的时候看到她被一群大小不一的狗簇拥着穿过花园，像一个毛茸茸的移动马戏团。有一天我跟她聊起来，发现她对人类行为学有一套自己的理论，全部从遛狗中总结出来。她说：「你想了解一个人的真实性格，不用跟他说话，看他养的狗就行。焦虑的人养小型犬，因为小狗的紧张感跟他们同频；控制欲强的人养训练度高的品种，比如德牧或边牧；真正孤独的人养大型懒狗，金毛或者拉布拉多，因为他们需要的不是互动，而是一个温暖的、不说话也不走开的存在。」我问她最难遛的是什么狗，她不假思索地说：「没有难遛的狗，只有难理解的主人。狗的问题百分之九十都是主人的投射。一只焦虑的狗背后几乎总有一个焦虑的家庭。」她还说每只狗有自己的散步哲学——有的喜欢探索每一个角落，有的只走固定路线，有的走三步就要停下来闻一闻。「跟人一样，」她笑着说，「有的人旅行是为了发现新东西，有的只是为了确认旧东西还在。」
text_pinyin: Xiaoqu li you yi wei zhiye liu gou shi, ta jiao A Qiu, mei tian tongshi liu liu qi zhi butong renjia de gou. Wo jingchang zai bangwan de shihou kan dao ta bei yi qun daxiao buyi de gou cuyong zhe chuanguo huayuan, xiang yi ge maorongrong de yidong maxituan. You yi tian wo gen ta liao qilai, faxian ta dui renlei xingweixue you yi tao ziji de lilun, quanbu cong liu gou zhong zongjie chulai. Ta shuo: 'Ni xiang liaojie yi ge ren de zhenshi xingge, bu yong gen ta shuo hua, kan ta yang de gou jiu xing. Jiaolv de ren yang xiaoxing quan, yinwei xiaogou de jinzhang gan gen tamen tongpin; kongzhiyu qiang de ren yang xunliandu gao de pinzhong, biru demu huo bianmu; zhenzheng gudu de ren yang daxing lan gou, jinmao huozhe Labuladuo, yinwei tamen xuyao de bu shi hudong, er shi yi ge wennuan de, bu shuo hua ye bu zou kai de cunzai.' Wo wen ta zui nan liu de shi shenme gou, ta bu jia sisuo de shuo: 'Meiyou nan liu de gou, zhi you nan lijie de zhuren. Gou de wenti baifenzhi jiushi dou shi zhuren de toushe. Yi zhi jiaolv de gou beihou jihu zong you yi ge jiaolv de jiating.' Ta hai shuo mei zhi gou you ziji de sanbu zhexue—you de xihuan tansuo mei yi ge jiaoluo, you de zhi zou guding luxian, you de zou san bu jiu yao ting xialai wen yi wen. 'Gen ren yiyang,' ta xiao zhe shuo, 'you de ren lxing shi weile faxian xin dongxi, you de zhi shi weile queren jiu dongxi hai zai.'
text_en: There's a professional dog walker in our community named Ah Qiu, who walks six or seven dogs from different families simultaneously each day. I often see her in the evening surrounded by dogs of various sizes crossing the garden, like a fuzzy mobile circus. One day I chatted with her and discovered she has her own theory of human behavior, entirely derived from dog walking. She says: 'If you want to understand someone's true personality, don't talk to them—just look at their dog. Anxious people keep small dogs because the small dog's nervousness resonates with theirs. Control-oriented people keep highly trainable breeds like German or Border Shepherds. Truly lonely people keep large lazy dogs—Golden Retrievers or Labradors—because what they need isn't interaction but a warm presence that neither speaks nor leaves.' I asked which dogs are hardest to walk. Without hesitation she said: 'There are no hard-to-walk dogs, only hard-to-understand owners. Ninety percent of dog problems are projections from the owner. Behind an anxious dog is almost always an anxious household.' She added that each dog has its own walking philosophy—some explore every corner, some only follow fixed routes, some stop to sniff every three steps. 'Same as people,' she said with a smile. 'Some people travel to discover new things; some just want to confirm the old things are still there.'

title: The Barber Who Remembers
text_zh: 我去同一家理发店理了十二年的发。理发师老何从来不问我「今天想怎么剪」，因为他记得我每一次的发型偏好，甚至记得我在什么时期喜欢留长一点，什么时期会要求剪短。他说他对所有固定客人都这样——不是故意去记，而是手的肌肉记忆比大脑更忠实。他的手碰到一个人的头发，就会自动调取上次的剪法和力度。更神奇的是他的职业直觉：他能从头发的质地变化判断客人最近的生活状态。他跟我说过：「压力大的人头发会变硬变干，刚失恋的人来剪头发通常会要求大幅度改变——好像换个发型就能换个人。生了孩子的女人头发变薄，不是因为营养不良，是因为她们睡眠不够身体在重新分配资源。」有一次他给一位老客人剪头发的时候突然变得格外温柔，事后我问他为什么，他说：「他的头发最近掉得很厉害，可能在做化疗。但他没有说，我也不问。我只是剪得更轻一些，让他觉得自己的头发还是完好的。」那一刻我觉得理发师也是一种治疗师——他们不治身体也不治心灵，他们治的是人对自己外表的那一点点信心。
text_pinyin: Wo qu tong yi jia lifadian li le shi'er nian de fa. Lifashi Lao He conglai bu wen wo 'jintian xiang zenme jian', yinwei ta jide wo mei yi ci de faxing pianhao, shenzhi jide wo zai shenme shiqi xihuan liu chang yi dian, shenme shiqi hui yaoqiu jian duan. Ta shuo ta dui suoyou guding keren dou zheyang—bu shi guyi qu ji, er shi shou de jirou jiyi bi danao geng zhongshi. Ta de shou peng dao yi ge ren de toufa, jiu hui zidong diaqu shang ci de jianfa he lidu. Geng shenqi de shi ta de zhiye zhijue: ta neng cong toufa de zhidi bianhua panduan keren zuijin de shenghuo zhuangtai. Ta gen wo shuo guo: 'Yali da de ren toufa hui bian ying bian gan, gang shilian de ren lai jian toufa tongchang hui yaoqiu da fudu gaibian—haoxiang huan ge faxing jiu neng huan ge ren. Sheng le haizi de nvren toufa bian bao, bu shi yinwei yingyang buliang, shi yinwei tamen shuimian bu gou shenti zai chongxin fenpei ziyuan.' You yi ci ta gei yi wei lao keren jian toufa de shihou turan bian de gewai wenrou, shihou wo wen ta weishenme, ta shuo: 'Ta de toufa zuijin diao de hen lihai, keneng zai zuo huanliao. Dan ta meiyou shuo, wo ye bu wen. Wo zhi shi jian de geng qing yixie, rang ta juede ziji de toufa hai shi wanhao de.' Na yi ke wo juede lifashi ye shi yi zhong zhiliaoshi—tamen bu zhi shenti ye bu zhi xinling, tamen zhi de shi ren dui ziji waibiao de na yi dian dian xinxin.
text_en: I've been getting my hair cut at the same barbershop for twelve years. Barber Old He never asks 'how would you like it today' because he remembers my style preference every time, even recalls during which period I liked it longer and when I'd ask for it short. He says he's like this with all regular clients—not deliberately memorizing, but his hands' muscle memory is more faithful than the brain. When his hands touch someone's hair, they automatically retrieve the previous cut and pressure. Even more remarkable is his professional intuition: he can judge a client's recent life state from changes in hair texture. He's told me: 'Stressed people's hair becomes stiff and dry. People who just broke up usually request dramatic changes—as if a new hairstyle could make them a new person. Women who've had babies get thinner hair, not from malnutrition but because insufficient sleep makes the body redistribute resources.' Once he suddenly became especially gentle while cutting a regular client's hair. Afterward I asked why. He said: 'His hair's been falling out badly lately—probably chemotherapy. But he didn't say, and I don't ask. I just cut more gently, letting him feel his hair is still intact.' In that moment I felt barbers are also a kind of therapist—they don't treat bodies or minds; they treat that small bit of confidence people have in their own appearance.

title: The Window Cleaner's View
text_zh: 高楼外墙的擦窗工人每天从三十层的高度俯瞰城市，但我发现他们从不谈论景色。我有一次在天台遇到正在休息的擦窗工老赵，问他从那么高的地方看下去是什么感觉。他说：「看了十几年了，早就不看风景了。我看的是窗户。」他说每一扇窗户都是一个微型舞台，他从外面看进去能看到人们以为没人看到的样子——有人在办公室里偷偷哭，有人对着镜子练习演讲，有人把午饭藏在抽屉里吃，有人在视频会议的时候下半身穿着睡裤。「你们以为窗户是透明的，是用来看外面的，」他说，「但从我的角度来看，窗户是用来看里面的。每个人在窗户后面都比在门外面更真实。」他特别提到过一个场景：一个中年男人每天下午三点会站在靠窗的位置发呆五分钟，然后回到工位继续工作。老赵擦了那栋楼三年，那个男人也发了三年的呆，每次都是同一个方向、同一个表情。「我不知道他在想什么，」老赵说，「但那五分钟的发呆大概是他一天里唯一属于自己的时间。」我忽然觉得，擦窗工也许是城市里最不被注意的窥视者——他们看到了我们最真实的样子，然后擦掉玻璃上的灰尘，什么都不说。
text_pinyin: Gaolou waiqiang de ca chuang gongren mei tian cong sanshi ceng de gaodu fukan chengshi, dan wo faxian tamen cong bu tanlun jingse. Wo you yi ci zai tiantai yu dao zhengzai xiuxi de ca chuang gong Lao Zhao, wen ta cong name gao de difang kan xiaqu shi shenme ganjue. Ta shuo: 'Kan le shi ji nian le, zao jiu bu kan fengjing le. Wo kan de shi chuanghu.' Ta shuo mei yi shan chuanghu dou shi yi ge weixing wutai, ta cong waimian kan jinqu neng kan dao renmen yiwei mei ren kan dao de yangzi—you ren zai bangongshi li toutou ku, you ren dui zhe jingzi lianxi yanjiang, you ren ba wufan cang zai chouti li chi, you ren zai shipin huiyi de shihou xiabanshen chuan zhe shuiku. 'Nimen yiwei chuanghu shi touming de, shi yonglai kan waimian de,' ta shuo, 'dan cong wo de jiaodu lai kan, chuanghu shi yonglai kan limian de. Mei ge ren zai chuanghu houmian dou bi zai men waimian geng zhenshi.' Ta tebie ti dao guo yi ge changjing: yi ge zhongnian nanren mei tian xiawu san dian hui zhan zai kao chuang de weizhi fadai wu fenzhong, ranhou hui dao gongwei jixu gongzuo. Lao Zhao ca le na dong lou san nian, nage nanren ye fa le san nian de dai, mei ci dou shi tong yi ge fangxiang, tong yi ge biaoqing. 'Wo bu zhidao ta zai xiang shenme,' Lao Zhao shuo, 'dan na wu fenzhong de fadai dagai shi ta yi tian li weiyi shuyu ziji de shijian.' Wo huran juede, ca chuang gong yexu shi chengshi li zui bu bei zhuyi de kuishi zhe—tamen kan dao le women zui zhenshi de yangzi, ranhou ca diao boli shang de huichen, shenme dou bu shuo.
text_en: High-rise window cleaners look down on the city from thirty stories every day, but I've found they never talk about the view. I once met window cleaner Old Zhao resting on a rooftop and asked what it feels like looking down from that height. He said: 'After over a decade, I stopped looking at scenery long ago. I look at windows.' He said every window is a miniature stage; looking in from outside he sees people as they think no one is watching—someone crying secretly in the office, someone practicing a speech in the mirror, someone hiding lunch in a desk drawer to eat, someone wearing pajama pants below the frame during a video call. 'You think windows are transparent, meant for looking outside,' he said, 'but from my angle, windows are for looking inside. Everyone is more real behind a window than outside a door.' He specifically mentioned one scene: a middle-aged man stands by the window at 3 PM every day and zones out for five minutes, then returns to his desk to continue working. Old Zhao cleaned that building for three years, and the man zoned out for three years—always the same direction, same expression. 'I don't know what he's thinking,' Old Zhao said, 'but those five minutes of spacing out are probably the only time in his day that belongs to him.' I suddenly felt that window cleaners may be the city's most unnoticed voyeurs—they see our most authentic selves, then wipe the dust from the glass and say nothing.

title: The Sound of Chopsticks
text_zh: 如果你闭着眼睛坐在一家中餐馆里，光听筷子的声音就能还原出大量信息。筷子夹菜的声音清脆而短促，像雨滴打在竹叶上；筷子碰到碗沿的声音是一种瓷器特有的薄脆回响；搅拌汤碗的声音浑厚而缓慢，透着一种不紧不慢的踏实。我在一次停电的晚餐中意外发现了这个声音世界。那天餐厅突然断电，黑暗中所有的视觉信息都消失了，但声音变得异常清晰。我能听出邻桌是几个人在吃饭——三双筷子，节奏不同，其中一双明显慢很多，大概是老人。有一桌传来频繁的碰杯声和笑声，应该是在庆祝什么。最远处那桌只有一双筷子的声音，间或夹杂着手机屏幕被点亮的微弱光芒——一个人吃饭，边吃边看手机。电恢复以后我环顾四周，发现听觉描绘出的画面跟视觉完全吻合。那个晚上让我意识到，我们对世界的感知有多依赖眼睛，又有多忽略耳朵。一双筷子的声音里包含着速度、力度、材质、心情，它是一种比语言更古老的表达方式——不说话的时候，筷子替我们说了。
text_pinyin: Ruguo ni bi zhe yanjing zuo zai yi jia Zhongcanguan li, guang ting kuaizi de shengyin jiu neng huanyuan chu daliang xinxi. Kuaizi jia cai de shengyin qingcui er duancu, xiang yudi da zai zhuye shang; kuaizi peng dao wan yan de shengyin shi yi zhong ciqi teyou de baocui huixiang; jiaoban tangwan de shengyin hunhou er huanman, tou zhe yi zhong bu jin bu man de tashi. Wo zai yi ci tingdian de wancan zhong yiwai faxian le zhege shengyin shijie. Na tian canting turan duandian, hei'an zhong suoyou de shijue xinxi dou xiaoshi le, dan shengyin bian de yichang qingxi. Wo neng ting chu linzhuo shi ji ge ren zai chifan—san shuang kuaizi, jiezou butong, qizhong yi shuang mingxian man hen duo, dagai shi laoren. You yi zhuo chuan lai pinpan de pengbei sheng he xiao sheng, yinggai shi zai qingzhu shenme. Zui yuanchu na zhuo zhi you yi shuang kuaizi de shengyin, jianhuo jiaza zhe shouji pingmu bei dian liang de weiruo guangmang—yi ge ren chifan, bian chi bian kan shouji. Dian huifu yihou wo huangu sizhou, faxian tingjue miaohu chu de huamian gen shijue wanquan wenhe. Nage wanshang rang wo yishi dao, women dui shijie de ganzhi you duo yilai yanjing, you you duo hulue erduo. Yi shuang kuaizi de shengyin li baohanzhe sudu, lidu, caizhi, xinqing, ta shi yi zhong bi yuyan geng gulao de biaoda fangshi—bu shuohua de shihou, kuaizi ti women shuo le.
text_en: If you sit in a Chinese restaurant with your eyes closed, the sound of chopsticks alone can reconstruct enormous amounts of information. Chopsticks picking up food make a crisp, brief sound, like raindrops on bamboo leaves; chopsticks touching a bowl rim produce a thin, resonant ring unique to porcelain; stirring a soup bowl is deep and slow, conveying an unhurried steadiness. I accidentally discovered this sonic world during a power outage dinner. The restaurant suddenly lost power; in the darkness all visual information vanished, but sounds became extraordinarily clear. I could hear how many people were eating at the next table—three pairs of chopsticks, different rhythms, one noticeably slower, probably elderly. Another table had frequent clinking glasses and laughter, probably celebrating something. The farthest table had only one pair of chopsticks, occasionally interspersed with the faint glow of a phone screen being lit—someone eating alone, watching their phone while eating. After power returned I looked around and found the picture drawn by hearing perfectly matched the visual. That evening made me realize how dependent our perception of the world is on eyes, and how much we neglect ears. A pair of chopsticks' sound contains speed, pressure, material, and mood—it's an expression older than language; when we're not speaking, chopsticks speak for us.

title: The Taxi Driver's Philosophy
text_zh: 我坐过一辆出租车，司机是个话不多的中年人，车里放着古典音乐——不是为了附庸风雅，他说是因为古典乐没有歌词，不会让他想跟着唱，这样开车更安全。全程我们几乎没说话，只在快到目的地的时候他忽然说了一句：「我开了二十年出租车，拉过的人大概有几十万了。你知道什么人让我印象最深吗？不是明星，不是有钱人，是那些上车以后告诉我'不急，绕远路也行'的人。」他说这种乘客极其罕见，二十年里不超过十个。但每一个说出这句话的人，后来都会在车上说一些很深的话——也许是因为他们放弃了效率，时间就变得奢侈了，在奢侈的时间里人总是更容易打开自己。「我觉得一座城市的速度就是被出租车这个行业定义的，」他说，「每一个乘客上车第一句话都是'快点'或者报一个目的地。目的地和速度，这就是现代人的全部了吧。」他在红灯前停下来，看了一眼计价器说：「偶尔没有目的地地开一会儿，其实也不错。」我没有回答，因为那个瞬间我觉得计价器跳动的数字像一首静默的诗——它在用最精确的方式丈量着一段即将结束的、两个陌生人之间的短暂相遇。
text_pinyin: Wo zuo guo yi liang chuzuche, siji shi ge hua bu duo de zhongnian ren, che li fang zhe gudian yinyue—bu shi weile fuyong fengya, ta shuo shi yinwei gudianle meiyou geci, bu hui rang ta xiang gen zhe chang, zheyang kaiche geng anquan. Quancheng women jihu mei shuo hua, zhi zai kuai dao mudidi de shihou ta huran shuo le yi ju: 'Wo kai le ershi nian chuzuche, la guo de ren dagai you ji shi wan le. Ni zhidao shenme ren rang wo yinxiang zui shen ma? Bu shi mingxing, bu shi you qian ren, shi naxie shang che yihou gaosu wo bu ji, rao yuanlu ye xing de ren.' Ta shuo zhe zhong chengke jiqi handjian, ershi nian li bu chaoguo shi ge. Dan mei yi ge shuo chu zhe ju hua de ren, houlai dou hui zai che shang shuo yixie hen shen de hua—yexu shi yinwei tamen fangqi le xiaolv, shijian jiu bian de shechi le, zai shechi de shijian li ren zong shi geng rongyi dakai ziji. 'Wo juede yi zuo chengshi de sudu jiu shi bei chuzuche zhege hangye dingyi de,' ta shuo, 'mei yi ge chengke shang che di yi ju hua dou shi kuai dian huozhe bao yi ge mudidi. Mudidi he sudu, zhe jiu shi xiandai ren de quanbu le ba.' Ta zai hongdeng qian ting xialai, kan le yi yan jijiqi shuo: 'Ou'er meiyou mudidi de kai yihuir, qishi ye bu cuo.' Wo meiyou huida, yinwei nage shunjian wo juede jijiqi tiaodong de shuzi xiang yi shou jingmo de shi—ta zai yong zui jingque de fangshi zhangliang zhe yi duan jijiang jieshu de, liang ge moshengren zhijian de duanzan xiangyu.
text_en: I once rode a taxi whose driver was a quiet middle-aged man with classical music playing—not for pretension; he said it's because classical music has no lyrics, so he won't want to sing along, making driving safer. We barely spoke the entire ride, until near the destination he suddenly said: 'I've driven a taxi for twenty years, carried maybe hundreds of thousands of people. Know who impressed me most? Not celebrities, not rich people—it's those who get in and say, I'm not in a hurry, taking the long way is fine.' He said such passengers are extremely rare—under ten in twenty years. But every person who said this would later say something profound in the car—perhaps because they'd abandoned efficiency, time became luxurious, and in luxurious time people are more easily opened. 'I think a city's speed is defined by the taxi industry,' he said. 'Every passenger's first words are either hurry up or a destination name. Destination and speed—that's all modern people are, isn't it?' He stopped at a red light, glanced at the meter and said: 'Driving without a destination once in a while isn't bad actually.' I didn't respond, because in that moment I felt the meter's ticking numbers were like a silent poem—measuring with utmost precision a soon-ending, brief encounter between two strangers.

title: The Umbrella Repairman
text_zh: 菜市场门口有一个修伞的老头，摊子小到几乎看不见——一张折叠凳，一个装满零件的铁盒，手边散落着几根弯了的伞骨。在一个雨伞十五块钱一把的年代，修伞这门手艺的存在本身就是一种温和的抗议。我有一把用了七年的折叠伞，伞骨断了一根，扔掉觉得可惜，拿去给他修。他接过伞翻看了几下，说：「好伞。现在的伞都是一次性的，你这把的骨架用的是钢丝，能修。」他修伞的时候我蹲在旁边看，发现他的手指上缠满了创可贴——伞骨的铁丝尖端锋利，不小心就会扎到手。十五分钟后伞修好了，他收了我八块钱。我问他一天能修几把伞，他说好的时候七八把，差的时候一两把都没有。算下来月收入恐怕还不如在工厂打工。他不在乎：「我老伴退休金够我们两个人花的。修伞不是为了赚钱，是因为我这辈子就会这一样东西，不做的话手闲着难受。」我打着修好的伞走进雨里，忽然觉得手里的伞变重了——不是物理上的重量，而是因为它多了一段被修补过的故事。也许在一个鼓励你丢掉旧的买新的世界里，愿意修补的人才是真正的奢侈品。
text_pinyin: Caishichang menkou you yi ge xiu san de laotou, tanzi xiao dao jihu kan bu jian—yi zhang zhedie deng, yi ge zhuangman lingjian de tie he, shou bian sanluo zhe ji gen wan le de sangu. Zai yi ge yusan shiwu kuai qian yi ba de niandai, xiu san zhe men shouyi de cunzai benshen jiu shi yi zhong wenhe de kangyi. Wo you yi ba yong le qi nian de zhedie san, sangu duan le yi gen, reng diao juede kexi, na qu gei ta xiu. Ta jie guo san fankan le ji xia, shuo: 'Hao san. Xianzai de san dou shi yicixing de, ni zhe ba de gujia yong de shi gangsi, neng xiu.' Ta xiu san de shihou wo dun zai pangbian kan, faxian ta de shouzhi shang chan man le chuangketie—sangu de tiesi jianduan fengdli, bu xiaoxin jiu hui zha dao shou. Shiwu fenzhong hou san xiu hao le, ta shou le wo ba kuai qian. Wo wen ta yi tian neng xiu ji ba san, ta shuo hao de shihou qi ba ba, cha de shihou yi liang ba dou meiyou. Suan xialai yue shouru kongpa hai buru zai gongchang dagong. Ta bu zaihu: 'Wo laoban tuixiujin gou women liang ge ren hua de. Xiu san bu shi weile zhuan qian, shi yinwei wo zhebeizi jiu hui zhe yi yang dongxi, bu zuo de hua shou xian zhe nanshou.' Wo da zhe xiu hao de san zou jin yu li, huran juede shou li de san bian zhong le—bu shi wuli shang de zhongliang, er shi yinwei ta duo le yi duan bei xiubu guo de gushi. Yexu zai yi ge guli ni diu diao jiu de mai xin de shijie li, yuanyi xiubu de ren cai shi zhenzheng de shechipin.
text_en: There's an old man who repairs umbrellas at the wet market entrance, his stand so small it's nearly invisible—a folding stool, a tin box full of parts, a few bent umbrella ribs scattered nearby. In an era when umbrellas cost fifteen yuan, the very existence of umbrella repair is a gentle protest. I had a folding umbrella I'd used for seven years with one broken rib—felt wasteful to throw away, so I brought it to him. He took it, turned it over a few times, said: 'Good umbrella. Today's umbrellas are disposable; yours has a steel wire frame, repairable.' While he worked I squatted beside him watching, noticing his fingers were covered in band-aids—the wire tips of umbrella ribs are sharp and easily prick hands. Fifteen minutes later it was fixed; he charged eight yuan. I asked how many he repairs daily. Good days seven or eight, bad days one or two or none. His monthly income probably doesn't match factory work. He doesn't care: 'My wife's pension is enough for both of us. Repairing umbrellas isn't for money—it's because this is the only thing I know how to do in my life, and if I don't do it my hands feel restless.' I walked into the rain with the repaired umbrella, suddenly feeling it had grown heavier—not physical weight, but because it now carried an additional story of being mended. Perhaps in a world that encourages discarding the old and buying new, people willing to repair are the true luxury.

title: The School Janitor's Calendar
text_zh: 我小学门口的门卫室里挂着一本挂历，上面密密麻麻写满了字。门卫老刘在这所学校守了二十五年，那本挂历记录的不是他自己的日程，而是整所学校的非官方编年史。比如三月八号旁边写着「三年二班小胖摔断了腿」，九月一号写着「今年新生哭的比去年少」，十二月二十五号写着「有家长投诉圣诞节装饰不妥，校长让拆了」。翻开往年的挂历更有意思：2015年的某页写着「学校第一次装了监控摄像头，从此门卫的存在感降低百分之五十」，语气里透着自嘲。老刘说他记这些纯粹是习惯，没有任何目的。但当我把好几年的挂历串起来读的时候，居然能看出一些趋势：家长接送的时间逐年提前，说明竞争焦虑在加剧；学生戴眼镜的比例年年上升，他特别在2018年的挂历上画了个感叹号；课间在操场上跑的孩子越来越少，取而代之的是蹲在走廊里看手机的身影。一个门卫用挂历记录的碎片信息，拼凑出来的教育变迁图景，比任何官方的教育报告都更有温度。
text_pinyin: Wo xiaoxue menkou de menwei shi li gua zhe yi ben guali, shang mian mimimama xie man le zi. Menwei Lao Liu zai zhe suo xuexiao shou le ershiwu nian, na ben guali jilu de bu shi ta ziji de richeng, er shi zheng suo xuexiao de fei guanfang biannianshi. Biru san yue ba hao pangbian xie zhe 'san nian er ban xiao pang shuai duan le tui', jiu yue yi hao xie zhe 'jinnian xinsheng ku de bi qunian shao', shi'er yue ershiwu hao xie zhe 'you jiazhang tousu shengdanjie zhuangshi butuo, xiaozhang rang chai le'. Fankai wangnian de guali geng you yisi: 2015 nian de mou ye xie zhe 'xuexiao di yi ci zhuang le jiankong shexiangtou, congci menwei de cunzaigan jiangdi baifenzhi wushi', yuqi li tou zhe zichao. Lao Liu shuo ta ji zhexie chuncui shi xiguan, meiyou renhe mudi. Dan dang wo ba hao ji nian de guali chuan qilai du de shihou, jingran neng kan chu yixie qushi: jiazhang jiesong de shijian zhunian tiqian, shuoming jingzheng jiaolv zai jiaju; xuesheng dai yanjing de bili niannian shangsheng, ta tebie zai 2018 nian de guali shang hua le ge gantanhao; kejian zai caochang shang pao de haizi yuelaiyueshao, qu er dai zhi de shi dun zai zoulang li kan shouji de shenying. Yi ge menwei yong guali jilu de suipian xinxi, pincou chulai de jiaoyu bianqian tujing, bi renhe guanfang de jiaoyu baogao dou geng you wendu.
text_en: The guard booth at my elementary school entrance has a wall calendar densely covered in handwriting. Guard Old Liu has watched over this school for twenty-five years, and the calendar records not his own schedule but the school's unofficial chronicle. For instance, next to March 8th: 'Chubby kid in Class 3-2 broke his leg.' September 1st: 'This year's new students cried less than last year.' December 25th: 'A parent complained Christmas decorations were inappropriate; principal had them removed.' Past years' calendars are even more interesting: a 2015 page reads 'School installed surveillance cameras for the first time; guard's sense of existence reduced by 50%'—with a self-deprecating tone. Old Liu says he records these purely out of habit, with no purpose. But when I strung several years' calendars together, trends emerged: parents' pickup times advancing year by year, indicating escalating competitive anxiety; the proportion of students wearing glasses rising annually—he drew an exclamation mark on the 2018 calendar; fewer children running on the playground during breaks, replaced by figures squatting in hallways watching phones. A guard's fragmentary calendar information assembles a picture of educational change that has more warmth than any official education report.

title: The Courthouse Steps
text_zh: 我在法院门口的台阶上等朋友的时候，观察了半小时的人流。法院是一个极其特殊的公共空间——每个走进去的人都带着一个故事，而且通常不是好故事。你能从人们走上台阶的方式大致猜出他们的角色：律师们步伐稳健，通常手里夹着公文包，脸上是一种专业的冷静；原告和被告走得慢而犹豫，偶尔会在台阶中间停下来深呼吸，好像在积攒勇气；证人们往往走得最快，大概想尽快结束这件不是自己的事。最让我心酸的是那些一家人一起来的——离婚案件的当事人通常一前一后走，中间隔着足以容纳一段婚姻残骸的距离。有一对看起来刚吵完架的中年夫妻，女方在台阶上忽然蹲下来哭了。男方走了几步回头看了一眼，欲言又止，最终还是转身进了大门。那几秒钟的犹豫大概是这段关系最后的温度——再往前走一步就是法律程序，而法律不计算温度。法院台阶是一个微型的人性剧场：所有的爱恨纠葛在走进大门之前还是活的、混乱的、带体温的，走进去以后就会被翻译成条款、日期和判决书上干燥的字迹。
text_pinyin: Wo zai fayuan menkou de taijie shang deng pengyou de shihou, guancha le ban xiaoshi de renliu. Fayuan shi yi ge jiqi teshu de gonggong kongjian—mei ge zou jinqu de ren dou dai zhe yi ge gushi, erqie tongchang bu shi hao gushi. Ni neng cong renmen zou shang taijie de fangshi dazhi cai chu tamen de juese: lvshi men bufa wenjian, tongchang shou li jia zhe gongwenbao, lian shang shi yi zhong zhuanye de lengjing; yuangao he beigao zou de man er youyu, ou'er hui zai taijie zhongjian ting xialai shen huxi, haoxiang zai jizan yongqi; zhengren men wangwang zou de zui kuai, dagai xiang jinkuai jieshu zhe jian bu shi ziji de shi. Zui rang wo xinsuan de shi naxie yi jia ren yiqi lai de—lihun anjian de dangshiren tongchang yi qian yi hou zou, zhongjian ge zhe zuyi rongna yi duan hunyin canhai de juli. You yi dui kan qilai gang chao wan jia de zhongnian fuqi, nvfang zai taijie shang huran dun xialai ku le. Nanfang zou le ji bu huitou kan le yi yan, yu yan you zhi, zuizhong haishi zhuanshen jin le damen. Na ji miaozhong de youyu dagai shi zhe duan guanxi zuihou de wendu—zai wang qian zou yi bu jiu shi falv chengxu, er falv bu jisuan wendu. Fayuan taijie shi yi ge weixing de renxing juchang: suoyou de ai hen jiuge zai zou jin damen zhiqian hai shi huo de, hunluan de, dai tiwen de, zou jinqu yihou jiu hui bei fanyi cheng tiaokuan, riqi he panjiueshu shang ganzao de ziji.
text_en: While waiting for a friend on the courthouse steps, I observed the flow of people for half an hour. A courthouse is an extremely special public space—everyone walking in carries a story, and usually not a good one. You can roughly guess people's roles from how they climb the steps: lawyers walk with steady strides, typically carrying briefcases, faces showing professional composure; plaintiffs and defendants walk slowly and hesitantly, sometimes stopping mid-staircase to take deep breaths, as if gathering courage; witnesses tend to walk fastest, probably wanting to finish this matter that isn't theirs. Most heartbreaking are families arriving together—divorce case parties typically walk one in front, one behind, separated by a distance wide enough to hold the wreckage of a marriage. One middle-aged couple who appeared to have just finished fighting—the woman suddenly crouched on the steps and cried. The man walked a few steps, looked back, started to speak but stopped, and ultimately turned and entered the door. Those few seconds of hesitation were probably the last warmth of that relationship—one more step forward is legal procedure, and law doesn't calculate warmth. The courthouse steps are a miniature theater of human nature: all love, hate, and entanglements are still alive, chaotic, and warm-bodied before entering the door; after going in, they'll be translated into clauses, dates, and the dry writing on a verdict.

title: The Bookshelf That Rearranges Itself
text_zh: 我书房里的书架出了一个奇怪的问题：书的顺序会在我不知情的情况下改变。一开始我以为是自己记错了，但当我特意在某两本书之间放了一张书签作为标记后，第二天发现书签夹在了另外两本书中间。我独居，没有钥匙交给任何人，窗户长年关着。我排除了所有合理的解释以后开始认真对待这件事。我安装了一个微型摄像头对准书架，连续录了三天。回放的时候什么异常都没有——没有人进来过，书也没有移动。但第四天早上起来，书的顺序确实又变了，而摄像头的录像没有任何异常。我把这件事讲给一个做心理咨询的朋友听，她给了一个我意想不到的解释：「你是不是最近在梦游？有些人在深度睡眠中会执行白天想做但没做的事情。你是不是一直想重新整理书架？」我仔细想了想，确实——我已经计划整理书架好几个月了，但总是拖延。她建议我在卧室门上挂一个铃铛试试。果然，第三天夜里我被铃铛声惊醒，发现自己站在书房里，手里正拿着一本书。原来一直在深夜重新排列书架的人是我自己。清醒的我不断拖延的事，沉睡的我替我完成了——这大概是我听过的最诡异的「自律」方式。
text_pinyin: Wo shufang li de shujia chu le yi ge qiguai de wenti: shu de shunxu hui zai wo bu zhiqing de qingkuang xia gaibian. Yi kaishi wo yiwei shi ziji ji cuo le, dan dang wo teyi zai mou liang ben shu zhijian fang le yi zhang shuqian zuowei biaoji hou, di er tian faxian shuqian jia zai le lingwai liang ben shu zhongjian. Wo duju, meiyou yaoshi jiao gei renhe ren, chuanghu changnian guan zhe. Wo paichu le suoyou heli de jieshi yihou kaishi renzhen duidai zhe jian shi. Wo anzhuang le yi ge weixing shexiangtou dui zhun shujia, lianxu lu le san tian. Huifang de shihou shenme yichang dou meiyou—meiyou ren jin lai guo, shu ye meiyou yidong. Dan di si tian zaoshang qi lai, shu de shunxu queshi you bian le, er shexiangtou de luxiang meiyou renhe yichang. Wo ba zhe jian shi jiang gei yi ge zuo xinli zixun de pengyou ting, ta gei le yi ge wo yi xiang bu dao de jieshi: 'Ni shi bu shi zuijin zai mengyou? Youxie ren zai shendu shuimian zhong hui zhixing baitian xiang zuo dan mei zuo de shiqing. Ni shi bu shi yizhi xiang chongxin zhengli shujia?' Wo zixi xiang le xiang, queshi—wo yijing jihua zhengli shujia hao ji ge yue le, dan zongshi tuoyan. Ta jianyi wo zai woshi men shang gua yi ge lingtang shi shi. Guoran, di san tian yeli wo bei lingtang sheng jingxing, faxian ziji zhan zai shufang li, shou li zheng na zhe yi ben shu. Yuanlai yizhi zai shenye chongxin pailie shujia de ren shi wo ziji. Qingxing de wo buduan tuoyan de shi, chenshui de wo ti wo wancheng le—zhe dagai shi wo ting guo de zui guiyi de 'zilv' fangshi.
text_en: My study's bookshelf developed a strange problem: books rearrange themselves without my knowledge. At first I thought I misremembered, but after deliberately placing a bookmark between two specific books as a marker, the next day I found the bookmark between two entirely different books. I live alone, haven't given keys to anyone, and the windows are permanently closed. After eliminating all rational explanations, I took the matter seriously. I installed a tiny camera aimed at the bookshelf and recorded for three consecutive days. The playback showed nothing abnormal—no one entered, books didn't move. But on the fourth morning, the book order had indeed changed again, while the camera footage showed nothing. I told a psychologist friend about this, and she offered an unexpected explanation: 'Have you been sleepwalking recently? Some people in deep sleep perform tasks they wanted to do during the day but didn't. Have you been wanting to reorganize your bookshelf?' I thought carefully—indeed, I'd been planning to reorganize for months but kept procrastinating. She suggested I hang a bell on my bedroom door. Sure enough, on the third night the bell woke me and I found myself standing in the study, holding a book. The person rearranging the bookshelf at night was me all along. What my waking self kept postponing, my sleeping self completed for me—probably the most eerie form of 'self-discipline' I've ever heard of.

title: The Overpass at Dusk
text_zh: 每天傍晚六点，那座天桥上总会聚集一群卖唱的人。他们并不相识，却像是达成了某种默契，各自占据天桥的一角，互不干扰。一个弹吉他的中年男人唱着八十年代的老歌，声音沙哑而温暖。斜对面是一个拉二胡的老人，曲调悠长，像是从很远的地方飘来。天桥下面是车流不息的马路，喇叭声此起彼伏，但站在桥上的人们似乎听不见。下班的行人匆匆走过，偶尔有人停下脚步，往打开的琴盒里扔几枚硬币。弹吉他的男人会微微点头致谢，但并不中断演奏。有一次我在桥上站了整整一个小时，看着天色从橙红变成深蓝。城市的灯光渐次亮起，天桥上的歌声依旧。那一刻我忽然觉得，这座城市最柔软的部分，不在那些精心设计的商业街区，而在这些无人规划的角落里。卖唱的人收拾东西离开时，互相挥了挥手。明天同一时间，他们还会再来。
text_pinyin: Měitiān bàngwǎn liù diǎn, nà zuò tiānqiáo shàng zǒng huì jùjí yì qún màichàng de rén. Tāmen bìng bù xiāngshí, què xiàng shì dáchéng le mǒu zhǒng mòqì, gèzì zhànjù tiānqiáo de yì jiǎo, hù bù gānrǎo. Yí gè tán jítā de zhōngnián nánrén chàng zhe bāshí niándài de lǎo gē, shēngyīn shāyǎ ér wēnnuǎn. Xié duìmiàn shì yí gè lā èrhú de lǎorén, qǔdiào yōucháng, xiàng shì cóng hěn yuǎn de dìfang piāo lái. Tiānqiáo xiàmiàn shì chēliú bù xī de mǎlù, lǎba shēng cǐqǐbǐfú, dàn zhàn zài qiáo shàng de rénmen sìhū tīng bú jiàn. Xiàbān de xíngrén cōngcōng zǒu guò, ǒu'ěr yǒu rén tíng xià jiǎobù, wǎng dǎkāi de qínhé lǐ rēng jǐ méi yìngbì. Tán jítā de nánrén huì wēiwēi diǎntóu zhìxiè, dàn bìng bù zhōngduàn yǎnzòu. Yǒu yí cì wǒ zài qiáo shàng zhàn le zhěngzhěng yí gè xiǎoshí, kàn zhe tiānsè cóng chénghóng biàn chéng shēnlán. Chéngshì de dēngguāng jiàncì liàng qǐ, tiānqiáo shàng de gēshēng yījiù. Nà yí kè wǒ hūrán juéde, zhè zuò chéngshì zuì róuruǎn de bùfen, bú zài nàxiē jīngxīn shèjì de shāngyè jiēqū, ér zài zhèxiē wú rén guīhuà de jiǎoluò lǐ. Màichàng de rén shōushi dōngxi líkāi shí, hùxiāng huī le huī shǒu. Míngtiān tóng yí shíjiān, tāmen hái huì zài lái.
text_en: Every evening at six, a group of street performers gathers on that overpass. They don't know each other, yet seem to have reached some tacit understanding, each claiming a corner of the bridge without interfering. A middle-aged man strums a guitar, singing eighties songs in a hoarse, warm voice. Diagonally across, an old man plays the erhu, the melody long and lingering, as if drifting from somewhere far away. Below the overpass, traffic flows ceaselessly, horns rising and falling, but those standing on the bridge seem not to hear. Commuters hurry past, occasionally someone stopping to toss a few coins into an open guitar case. The guitarist nods slightly in thanks but doesn't interrupt his playing. Once I stood on the bridge for a full hour, watching the sky turn from orange to deep blue. The city lights came on one by one, the singing on the overpass continuing as before. In that moment I suddenly felt that the softest part of this city isn't in those carefully designed commercial districts, but in these unplanned corners. When the performers packed up and left, they waved to each other. Tomorrow at the same time, they would come again.

title: The Night Bus
text_zh: 坐末班公交的人都有一种特殊的疲惫感。不是那种急躁的累，而是一种放弃挣扎之后的松弛。十一点四十的公交车上，乘客稀稀落落，每个人都隔开好几个座位。靠窗的位置最受欢迎，大家把额头抵在玻璃上，看着窗外掠过的路灯。司机开得很稳，好像知道车上的人都不赶时间了。有一个穿工装的年轻人，手机屏幕亮着却没在看，眼神放空。对面坐着一个拎超市塑料袋的中年女人，袋子里露出一棵大白菜的叶子。她的脸上没有表情，但也不显得不快乐。最后一排坐着两个学生模样的人，小声讨论着什么。整辆车安静得能听见发动机的嗡嗡声。这种安静不令人不适，反而让人觉得被某种共同的倦意包裹着。每一站停下，车门打开，凉风涌进来，几个人默默下车，消失在夜色中。没有人说再见，也没有人需要说。末班车就是这样一个空间，所有人都卸下了白天的面具，允许自己只是安静地存在。
text_pinyin: Zuò mòbān gōngjiāo de rén dōu yǒu yì zhǒng tèshū de píbèi gǎn. Bú shì nà zhǒng jízào de lèi, ér shì yì zhǒng fàngqì zhēngzhá zhīhòu de sōngchí. Shíyī diǎn sìshí de gōngjiāo chē shàng, chéngkè xīxī-luòluò, měi gè rén dōu gé kāi hǎo jǐ gè zuòwèi. Kào chuāng de wèizhì zuì shòu huānyíng, dàjiā bǎ étóu dǐ zài bōli shàng, kàn zhe chuāng wài lüè guò de lùdēng. Sījī kāi de hěn wěn, hǎoxiàng zhīdào chē shàng de rén dōu bù gǎn shíjiān le. Yǒu yí gè chuān gōngzhuāng de niánqīng rén, shǒujī píngmù liàng zhe què méi zài kàn, yǎnshén fàng kōng. Duìmiàn zuò zhe yí gè līn chāoshì sùliào dài de zhōngnián nǚrén, dàizi lǐ lù chū yì kē dà báicài de yèzi. Tā de liǎn shàng méiyǒu biǎoqíng, dàn yě bù xiǎn de bú kuàilè. Zuìhòu yì pái zuò zhe liǎng gè xuéshēng múyàng de rén, xiǎo shēng tǎolùn zhe shénme. Zhěng liàng chē ānjìng de néng tīng jiàn fādòngjī de wēngwēng shēng. Zhè zhǒng ānjìng bú lìng rén bù shì, fǎn'ér ràng rén juéde bèi mǒu zhǒng gòngtóng de juànyì bāoguǒ zhe. Měi yí zhàn tíng xià, chēmén dǎkāi, liáng fēng yǒng jìnlái, jǐ gè rén mòmò xià chē, xiāoshī zài yèsè zhōng. Méiyǒu rén shuō zàijiàn, yě méiyǒu rén xūyào shuō. Mòbān chē jiù shì zhèyàng yí gè kōngjiān, suǒyǒu rén dōu xiè xià le báitiān de miànjù, yǔnxǔ zìjǐ zhǐ shì ānjìng de cúnzài.
text_en: People who ride the last bus share a particular kind of fatigue. Not the irritable sort, but a looseness that comes after giving up the struggle. On the 11:40 bus, passengers are scattered, each separated by several seats. Window seats are most popular, everyone pressing their forehead against the glass, watching the streetlights sweep past. The driver goes steadily, as if knowing nobody on board is in a hurry anymore. A young man in work coveralls has his phone screen lit but isn't looking at it, his gaze empty. Across the aisle sits a middle-aged woman carrying a supermarket bag, the leaves of a cabbage poking out. Her face shows no expression, but she doesn't seem unhappy either. In the back row sit two people who look like students, discussing something in low voices. The whole bus is so quiet you can hear the engine humming. This quiet isn't uncomfortable — rather, it makes you feel wrapped in some shared weariness. At each stop the doors open, cool air rushes in, a few people silently step off and vanish into the night. Nobody says goodbye, and nobody needs to. The last bus is that kind of space: everyone has taken off the masks of daytime, allowing themselves simply to exist in quietness.

title: The Locksmith's Shop
text_zh: 我家楼下有一个配钥匙的小摊，摊主是一个六十多岁的老师傅。他的摊子很小，只有一台切割机、几排空白钥匙坯和一把旧椅子。但他在那个位置已经待了快三十年。整条街的邻居换了一茬又一茬，只有他的摊子纹丝不动。他话不多，干活的时候专注得像在做手术。把钥匙夹在切割机上，对准样板，轻轻推进去，金属碎屑飞溅出来，嗡的一声之后，一把新钥匙就诞生了。他会拿起来对着光看一看，用指甲划过每个齿槽，确认没有毛刺。这套动作他大概做过几十万次了，但每次都一丝不苟。偶尔有人拿来很旧的钥匙，磨损得几乎看不清齿形，他就会戴上老花镜，把钥匙翻来覆去地看，然后凭经验手工修正。「配钥匙这活儿，」他有一次对我说，「看着简单，其实讲究一个精确。差一点点，锁就打不开。」我觉得他说的不只是钥匙的事。这个城市里有太多追求速度的人，而他只追求精确。时代在变，他的摊子却像一枚钉子，牢牢地钉在那里。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā lóu xià yǒu yí gè pèi yàoshi de xiǎo tān, tānzhǔ shì yí gè liùshí duō suì de lǎo shīfu. Tā de tānzi hěn xiǎo, zhǐ yǒu yì tái qiēgējī, jǐ pái kòngbái yàoshi pī hé yì bǎ jiù yǐzi. Dàn tā zài nàge wèizhì yǐjīng dāi le kuài sānshí nián. Zhěng tiáo jiē de línjū huàn le yí chá yòu yí chá, zhǐ yǒu tā de tānzi wénsī bú dòng. Tā huà bù duō, gànhuó de shíhou zhuānzhù de xiàng zài zuò shǒushù. Bǎ yàoshi jiā zài qiēgējī shàng, duìzhǔn yàngbǎn, qīngqīng tuī jìnqù, jīnshǔ suìxiè fēijiàn chūlái, wēng de yì shēng zhīhòu, yì bǎ xīn yàoshi jiù dànshēng le. Tā huì ná qǐlái duì zhe guāng kàn yi kàn, yòng zhǐjia huá guò měi gè chǐcáo, quèrèn méiyǒu máocì. Zhè tào dòngzuò tā dàgài zuò guò jǐ shí wàn cì le, dàn měi cì dōu yìsī bù gǒu. Ǒu'ěr yǒu rén ná lái hěn jiù de yàoshi, mósǔn de jīhū kàn bù qīng chǐxíng, tā jiù huì dài shàng lǎohuā jìng, bǎ yàoshi fānlái-fùqù de kàn, ránhòu píng jīngyàn shǒugōng xiūzhèng. 「Pèi yàoshi zhè huór,」 tā yǒu yí cì duì wǒ shuō, 「kàn zhe jiǎndān, qíshí jiǎngjiu yí gè jīngquè. Chà yìdiǎndiǎn, suǒ jiù dǎ bù kāi.」 Wǒ juéde tā shuō de bù zhǐ shì yàoshi de shì. Zhège chéngshì lǐ yǒu tài duō zhuīqiú sùdù de rén, ér tā zhǐ zhuīqiú jīngquè. Shídài zài biàn, tā de tānzi què xiàng yì méi dīngzi, láoláo de dīng zài nàlǐ.
text_en: Downstairs from my apartment is a small key-cutting stall, run by a master craftsman in his sixties. His stall is tiny — just a cutting machine, a few rows of blank key blanks, and an old chair. But he has been in that spot for nearly thirty years. The neighbors on the entire street have turned over again and again; only his stall hasn't budged. He doesn't talk much, focusing on his work like a surgeon in operation. He clamps the key in the cutting machine, aligns it with the template, gently pushes it forward, metal shavings fly out, and after a hum, a new key is born. He holds it up to the light, runs a fingernail over each groove to check for burrs. He has probably performed this sequence of movements hundreds of thousands of times, yet each time he is meticulous. Occasionally someone brings a very old key, worn down until the teeth are barely visible, and he puts on his reading glasses, turns the key over and over, then makes corrections by hand based on experience. 'Key-cutting,' he told me once, 'looks simple, but really it comes down to precision. Off by just a little, and the lock won't open.' I felt he wasn't only talking about keys. This city is full of people chasing speed, but he only chases precision. Times change, but his stall is like a nail, firmly driven in place.

title: The Missing Bookmark
text_zh: 图书馆的管理员发现了一件奇怪的事。最近两个月，陆陆续续有读者反映，他们夹在书里的书签不见了。不是那种普通的纸片书签，而是有一定收藏价值的——手工刺绣的、压花的、甚至有几张是古旧书店买的限量版。起初管理员以为是其他读者顺手拿走的，但仔细调查之后发现了一个规律：丢失书签的书全都来自三楼的文学阅览室，而且全是外国文学区的书。更奇怪的是，书签消失之后，原来夹书签的位置会多出一张白色小纸条，上面用钢笔工整地写着那一页中某个句子的中文翻译。管理员把所有纸条收集起来，发现这些被翻译的句子都有一个共同主题——关于离别。她开始留意三楼的常客。一个总是坐在靠窗位置的老先生引起了她的注意。他每天下午两点准时到，四点准时离开，永远只看外国文学。有一天，管理员悄悄走到他身后，看见他正小心翼翼地把一枚绣着梅花的书签夹进自己的笔记本。她没有立刻上前，而是等他离开后查看了他留在桌上的纸条。上面写着：「所有的离别，都是为了重逢。」她忽然不确定自己是否应该去追究了。
text_pinyin: Túshūguǎn de guǎnlǐyuán fāxiàn le yí jiàn qíguài de shì. Zuìjìn liǎng gè yuè, lùlù-xùxù yǒu dúzhě fǎnyìng, tāmen jiā zài shū lǐ de shūqiān bú jiàn le. Bú shì nà zhǒng pǔtōng de zhǐpiàn shūqiān, ér shì yǒu yídìng shōucáng jiàzhí de — shǒugōng cìxiù de, yāhuā de, shènzhì yǒu jǐ zhāng shì gǔjiù shūdiàn mǎi de xiànliàng bǎn. Qǐchū guǎnlǐyuán yǐwéi shì qítā dúzhě shùnshǒu ná zǒu de, dàn zǐxì diàochá zhīhòu fāxiàn le yí gè guīlǜ: diūshī shūqiān de shū quán dōu lái zì sān lóu de wénxué yuèlǎnshì, érqiě quán shì wàiguó wénxué qū de shū. Gèng qíguài de shì, shūqiān xiāoshī zhīhòu, yuánlái jiā shūqiān de wèizhì huì duō chū yì zhāng báisè xiǎo zhǐtiáo, shàngmiàn yòng gāngbǐ gōngzhěng de xiě zhe nà yí yè zhōng mǒu gè jùzi de Zhōngwén fānyì. Guǎnlǐyuán bǎ suǒyǒu zhǐtiáo shōují qǐlái, fāxiàn zhèxiē bèi fānyì de jùzi dōu yǒu yí gè gòngtóng zhǔtí — guānyú líbié. Tā kāishǐ liúyì sān lóu de chángkè. Yí gè zǒng shì zuò zài kào chuāng wèizhì de lǎo xiānsheng yǐnqǐ le tā de zhùyì. Tā měitiān xiàwǔ liǎng diǎn zhǔnshí dào, sì diǎn zhǔnshí líkāi, yǒngyuǎn zhǐ kàn wàiguó wénxué. Yǒu yì tiān, guǎnlǐyuán qiāoqiāo zǒu dào tā shēnhòu, kàn jiàn tā zhèng xiǎoxīn-yìyì de bǎ yì méi xiù zhe méihuā de shūqiān jiā jìn zìjǐ de bǐjìběn. Tā méiyǒu lìkè shàng qián, ér shì děng tā líkāi hòu chákàn le tā liú zài zhuō shàng de zhǐtiáo. Shàngmiàn xiě zhe: 「Suǒyǒu de líbié, dōu shì wèile chóngféng.」 Tā hūrán bú quèdìng zìjǐ shìfǒu yīnggāi qù zhuījiū le.
text_en: The library manager discovered something strange. Over the past two months, readers had been reporting, one after another, that bookmarks left in their books had gone missing. Not ordinary paper bookmarks, but ones with collecting value — hand-embroidered ones, pressed-flower ones, even a few limited editions from antique bookshops. At first the manager assumed other readers had taken them casually, but after careful investigation she found a pattern: all the books with missing bookmarks came from the third-floor literature reading room, and all from the foreign literature section. Stranger still, after a bookmark disappeared, a small white slip of paper would appear in its place, with a Chinese translation of a sentence from that page written neatly in fountain pen. The manager collected all the slips and found the translated sentences shared a common theme — parting. She began watching the third-floor regulars. An elderly gentleman who always sat by the window caught her attention. He arrived punctually at two every afternoon, left at four, and only ever read foreign literature. One day the manager quietly walked up behind him and saw him carefully slipping a plum-blossom-embroidered bookmark into his own notebook. She didn't approach immediately, but waited until he left and checked the slip he'd left on the table. It read: 'All partings are for the sake of reunion.' She suddenly wasn't sure whether she should pursue the matter further.

title: The Anonymous Donor
text_zh: 社区图书角的管理员注意到一个现象：每个月的第一天，书架上都会多出五本崭新的童书。没有人知道是谁放的。书的品味极好——不是那种粗制滥造的拼音读物，而是精心挑选的获奖绘本和经典童话，很多还是双语版。管理员在门口装了摄像头，但第二天发现摄像头的角度被人巧妙地调整过，刚好避开了书架。她换了一个隐蔽的位置，结果那天书架上没有出现新书，直到她把摄像头撤掉。她开始换一种思路。她注意到新书的封底都有一个小小的铅笔标记，像是一朵简笔画的向日葵。她去附近的书店询问，有没有人每月固定购买五本童书。一家独立书店的老板说有，是一个穿着朴素的中年女性，每次都用现金结账，从来不要发票。「她选书特别认真，」老板说，「有时候会在店里待一个多小时，把每本书都翻开看过。」管理员没有继续追查。她只是在书架旁边放了一个小本子，在第一页写了一句话：「谢谢您。孩子们很喜欢。」一个月后，小本子的第二页多了一行字：「这是我唯一能做的事。」字迹工整而克制，像是写了很久才写下的。
text_pinyin: Shèqū túshū jiǎo de guǎnlǐyuán zhùyì dào yí gè xiànxiàng: měi gè yuè de dì yī tiān, shūjià shàng dōu huì duō chū wǔ běn zhǎnxīn de tóngshū. Méiyǒu rén zhīdào shì shéi fàng de. Shū de pǐnwèi jí hǎo — bú shì nà zhǒng cūzhì-lànzào de pīnyīn dúwù, ér shì jīngxīn tiāoxuǎn de huòjiǎng huìběn hé jīngdiǎn tónghuà, hěn duō hái shì shuāngyǔ bǎn. Guǎnlǐyuán zài ménkǒu zhuāng le shèxiàngtóu, dàn dì èr tiān fāxiàn shèxiàngtóu de jiǎodù bèi rén qiǎomiào de tiáozhěng guò, gānghǎo bìkāi le shūjià. Tā huàn le yí gè yǐnbì de wèizhì, jiéguǒ nà tiān shūjià shàng méiyǒu chūxiàn xīn shū, zhídào tā bǎ shèxiàngtóu chè diào. Tā kāishǐ huàn yì zhǒng sīlù. Tā zhùyì dào xīn shū de fēngdǐ dōu yǒu yí gè xiǎo xiǎo de qiānbǐ biāojì, xiàng shì yì duǒ jiǎnbǐhuà de xiàngrìkuí. Tā qù fùjìn de shūdiàn xúnwèn, yǒu méi yǒu rén měi yuè gùdìng gòumǎi wǔ běn tóngshū. Yì jiā dúlì shūdiàn de lǎobǎn shuō yǒu, shì yí gè chuānzhuó púsù de zhōngnián nǚxìng, měi cì dōu yòng xiànjīn jiézhàng, cónglái bú yào fāpiào. 「Tā xuǎn shū tèbié rènzhēn,」 lǎobǎn shuō, 「yǒu shíhou huì zài diàn lǐ dāi yí gè duō xiǎoshí, bǎ měi běn shū dōu fānkāi kàn guò.」 Guǎnlǐyuán méiyǒu jìxù zhuīchá. Tā zhǐ shì zài shūjià pángbiān fàng le yí gè xiǎo běnzi, zài dì yī yè xiě le yí jù huà: 「Xièxie nín. Háizimen hěn xǐhuan.」 Yí gè yuè hòu, xiǎo běnzi de dì èr yè duō le yì háng zì: 「Zhè shì wǒ wéiyī néng zuò de shì.」 Zìjì gōngzhěng ér kèzhì, xiàng shì xiě le hěn jiǔ cái xiě xià de.
text_en: The manager of the community reading corner noticed a phenomenon: on the first day of every month, five brand-new children's books would appear on the shelf. Nobody knew who put them there. The taste in books was excellent — not cheaply made pinyin readers, but carefully selected award-winning picture books and classic fairy tales, many in bilingual editions. The manager installed a camera at the entrance, but the next day found its angle had been cleverly adjusted to avoid the bookshelf. She moved it to a hidden spot, but that day no new books appeared — not until she removed the camera. She tried a different approach. She noticed each new book had a small pencil mark on the back cover, like a simple sketch of a sunflower. She asked at nearby bookstores whether anyone regularly bought five children's books each month. An independent bookshop owner said yes — a plainly dressed middle-aged woman who always paid in cash and never asked for a receipt. 'She's very careful choosing books,' the owner said. 'Sometimes she stays over an hour, opening and checking every book.' The manager didn't investigate further. She simply placed a small notebook next to the shelf, writing on the first page: 'Thank you. The children love them.' A month later, the second page of the notebook had a new line: 'This is the only thing I can do.' The handwriting was neat and restrained, as if it had taken a long time to write.

title: The Accent
text_zh: 在北京生活了十五年之后，我以为自己的普通话已经完全没有口音了。直到有一天，出租车司机随口问了一句：「您是南方人吧？」我愣了一下，问他怎么听出来的。他说：「您说'是'的时候，舌头位置不太对。」那个晚上我躺在床上，反复对着天花板念「是」这个字，试图找到那个暴露我身份的细微差别。我来自一个说吴语方言的小城市，十八岁离开家乡去念大学，之后再也没有回去长住过。我曾经刻意抹掉口音，像是擦掉黑板上的旧字。但显然，有些痕迹是擦不干净的。我妈妈至今不会说普通话，每次打电话，我都要在两种语言之间切换。挂了电话之后，总需要几分钟才能完全切回普通话的频道。有一次开会，我不小心用方言说了一个语气词，同事们都笑了。我也跟着笑，但心里有一种说不清的感觉。口音是一条隐形的线，一头系着你已经离开的地方，一头系着你正在变成的人。你可以假装它不存在，但它会在最不经意的时候冒出来，提醒你，你并不完全属于任何一个地方。也许这才是口音真正要告诉我们的事。
text_pinyin: Zài Běijīng shēnghuó le shíwǔ nián zhīhòu, wǒ yǐwéi zìjǐ de pǔtōnghuà yǐjīng wánquán méiyǒu kǒuyīn le. Zhídào yǒu yì tiān, chūzūchē sījī suíkǒu wèn le yí jù: 「Nín shì nánfāng rén ba?」 Wǒ lèng le yíxià, wèn tā zěnme tīng chūlái de. Tā shuō: 「Nín shuō 'shì' de shíhou, shétou wèizhì bú tài duì.」 Nàge wǎnshang wǒ tǎng zài chuáng shàng, fǎnfù duì zhe tiānhuābǎn niàn 「shì」 zhège zì, shìtú zhǎo dào nàge bàolù wǒ shēnfèn de xìwēi chābié. Wǒ lái zì yí gè shuō Wúyǔ fāngyán de xiǎo chéngshì, shíbā suì líkāi jiāxiāng qù niàn dàxué, zhīhòu zài yě méiyǒu huíqu cháng zhù guò. Wǒ céngjīng kèyì mǒ diào kǒuyīn, xiàng shì cā diào hēibǎn shàng de jiù zì. Dàn xiǎnrán, yǒuxiē hénjì shì cā bù gānjìng de. Wǒ māma zhìjīn bú huì shuō pǔtōnghuà, měi cì dǎ diànhuà, wǒ dōu yào zài liǎng zhǒng yǔyán zhījiān qiēhuàn. Guà le diànhuà zhīhòu, zǒng xūyào jǐ fēnzhōng cái néng wánquán qiē huí pǔtōnghuà de píndào. Yǒu yí cì kāi huì, wǒ bù xiǎoxīn yòng fāngyán shuō le yí gè yǔqì cí, tóngshìmen dōu xiào le. Wǒ yě gēn zhe xiào, dàn xīnli yǒu yì zhǒng shuō bù qīng de gǎnjué. Kǒuyīn shì yì tiáo yǐnxíng de xiàn, yì tóu jì zhe nǐ yǐjīng líkāi de dìfang, yì tóu jì zhe nǐ zhèngzài biàn chéng de rén. Nǐ kěyǐ jiǎzhuāng tā bù cúnzài, dàn tā huì zài zuì bù jīngyì de shíhou mào chūlái, tíxǐng nǐ, nǐ bìng bù wánquán shǔyú rèn hé yí gè dìfang. Yěxǔ zhè cái shì kǒuyīn zhēnzhèng yào gàosu wǒmen de shì.
text_en: After living in Beijing for fifteen years, I thought my Mandarin was completely free of accent. Until one day a taxi driver casually asked, 'You're from the south, aren't you?' I was stunned for a moment and asked how he could tell. He said, 'When you say "shi," your tongue position isn't quite right.' That night I lay in bed, repeating 'shi' at the ceiling over and over, trying to find the subtle difference that had betrayed my identity. I come from a small city where Wu dialect is spoken. I left home at eighteen for university and never went back for any extended stay. I once deliberately erased my accent, like wiping old writing off a blackboard. But clearly, some traces can't be wiped clean. My mother still can't speak Mandarin; every phone call, I have to switch between two languages. After hanging up, it always takes a few minutes to fully switch back to the Mandarin channel. Once in a meeting, I accidentally used a dialect interjection, and my colleagues all laughed. I laughed along, but felt something I couldn't quite name. An accent is an invisible thread — one end tied to the place you've left, the other to the person you're becoming. You can pretend it doesn't exist, but it surfaces at the most unexpected moments, reminding you that you don't fully belong to any one place. Perhaps that is what an accent really wants to tell us.

title: The Inherited Recipe
text_zh: 外婆去世之后，我在她的抽屉里找到了一个泛黄的笔记本。里面记着她做的每一道菜的配方——用量精确到克，火候标注到分钟，甚至连「盐要最后放」这种细节都写得清清楚楚。我试着按照她的配方做了一道红烧肉。每一步都严格遵守，酱油三勺，糖一勺半，八角两颗，小火炖四十五分钟。出锅之后，味道很像，但总觉得差了一点什么。我又做了好几次，每次都微调一个变量，但始终无法完全复制那个味道。后来我翻到笔记本最后一页，发现外婆用铅笔写了一行很淡的字：「菜是给人吃的，不是给配方吃的。看着吃的人，你就知道该怎么做了。」我忽然明白了。外婆做菜的时候，总是一边炒一边看我们的表情。如果我皱了一下眉头，她会立刻加一点糖。如果我吃得太快，她会在下一道菜里多放一点料，让味道更浓一些。那些写在本子上的数字只是骨架，真正的调味是通过观察完成的。我现在也开始这样做菜了。不再盯着配方，而是看着对面吃饭的人。有时候我觉得，这也许就是外婆真正传给我的东西——不是一个配方，而是一种注意力。
text_pinyin: Wàipó qùshì zhīhòu, wǒ zài tā de chōuti lǐ zhǎo dào le yí gè fànhuáng de bǐjìběn. Lǐmiàn jì zhe tā zuò de měi yí dào cài de pèifāng — yòngliàng jīngquè dào kè, huǒhòu biāozhù dào fēnzhōng, shènzhì lián 「yán yào zuìhòu fàng」 zhè zhǒng xìjié dōu xiě de qīngqīng-chǔchǔ. Wǒ shì zhe ànzhào tā de pèifāng zuò le yí dào hóngshāo ròu. Měi yí bù dōu yángé zūnshǒu, jiàngyóu sān sháo, táng yì sháo bàn, bājiǎo liǎng kē, xiǎo huǒ dùn sìshíwǔ fēnzhōng. Chū guō zhīhòu, wèidào hěn xiàng, dàn zǒng juéde chà le yìdiǎn shénme. Wǒ yòu zuò le hǎo jǐ cì, měi cì dōu wēi tiáo yí gè biànliàng, dàn shǐzhōng wúfǎ wánquán fùzhì nàge wèidào. Hòulái wǒ fān dào bǐjìběn zuìhòu yí yè, fāxiàn wàipó yòng qiānbǐ xiě le yì háng hěn dàn de zì: 「Cài shì gěi rén chī de, bú shì gěi pèifāng chī de. Kàn zhe chī de rén, nǐ jiù zhīdào gāi zěnme zuò le.」 Wǒ hūrán míngbai le. Wàipó zuò cài de shíhou, zǒng shì yìbiān chǎo yìbiān kàn wǒmen de biǎoqíng. Rúguǒ wǒ zhòu le yíxià méitou, tā huì lìkè jiā yìdiǎn táng. Rúguǒ wǒ chī de tài kuài, tā huì zài xià yí dào cài lǐ duō fàng yìdiǎn liào, ràng wèidào gèng nóng yìxiē. Nàxiē xiě zài běnzi shàng de shùzì zhǐ shì gǔjià, zhēnzhèng de tiáowèi shì tōngguò guānchá wánchéng de. Wǒ xiànzài yě kāishǐ zhèyàng zuò cài le. Bú zài dīng zhe pèifāng, ér shì kàn zhe duìmiàn chīfàn de rén. Yǒu shíhou wǒ juéde, zhè yěxǔ jiù shì wàipó zhēnzhèng chuán gěi wǒ de dōngxi — bú shì yí gè pèifāng, ér shì yì zhǒng zhùyìlì.
text_en: After my grandmother passed away, I found a yellowed notebook in her drawer. It contained the recipe for every dish she made — quantities precise to the gram, heat levels noted to the minute, even details like 'add salt last' written out clearly. I tried making her braised pork following the recipe. Every step strictly followed: three spoonfuls of soy sauce, one and a half of sugar, two star anise, forty-five minutes on low heat. After plating, the taste was close, but something felt slightly off. I made it several more times, adjusting one variable each time, but could never fully replicate that flavor. Later I turned to the notebook's last page and found a line my grandmother had written faintly in pencil: 'Food is for people to eat, not for recipes to eat. Watch the person eating, and you'll know what to do.' I suddenly understood. When my grandmother cooked, she was always watching our expressions while stirring. If I frowned slightly, she would immediately add a bit of sugar. If I ate too fast, she would add more seasoning to the next dish, making the flavor richer. The numbers written in the notebook were just a skeleton; the real seasoning was accomplished through observation. I've started cooking this way too now. No longer staring at the recipe, but watching the person eating across from me. Sometimes I feel that perhaps this is what my grandmother truly passed down to me — not a recipe, but a kind of attentiveness.

title: The Office Plant
text_zh: 我们办公室有一盆绿萝，没有人知道它是谁买来的。它被放在打印机旁边的窗台上，位置极其尴尬——既挡着取打印件的路，又影响窗户的开关。但从来没有人主动把它移走。这盆绿萝的命运完全取决于谁坐在它旁边。前任坐在那个工位的小李每天给它浇两次水，结果根都泡烂了。后来换了老王，老王觉得植物就应该自然生长，连续三周没有浇水，绿萝的叶子全部变黄。行政部的小张看不下去，从网上查了养护方法，买了专用营养液，还给绿萝修了枝。绿萝终于恢复了生机，长出了新的藤蔓。小张为此颇为得意，在工作群里发了照片，标题是「行政部拯救绿萝」。结果第二天老板路过，说这个位置放植物不合规，让搬到休息区。小张把绿萝搬过去之后，再也没有人给它浇水了。一个月后，绿萝又奄奄一息。我私下觉得这盆绿萝的遭遇简直是一部微型的职场寓言——每个人都觉得它是别人的责任，偶尔有人出手相救，但制度总会在最不合时宜的时候出来干预。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen bàngōngshì yǒu yì pén lǜluó, méiyǒu rén zhīdào tā shì shéi mǎi lái de. Tā bèi fàng zài dǎyìnjī pángbiān de chuāngtái shàng, wèizhì jíqí gāngà — jì dǎng zhe qǔ dǎyìn jiàn de lù, yòu yǐngxiǎng chuānghù de kāiguān. Dàn cónglái méiyǒu rén zhǔdòng bǎ tā yí zǒu. Zhè pén lǜluó de mìngyùn wánquán qǔjué yú shéi zuò zài tā pángbiān. Qiánrèn zuò zài nàge gōngwèi de Xiǎo Lǐ měitiān gěi tā jiāo liǎng cì shuǐ, jiéguǒ gēn dōu pào làn le. Hòulái huàn le Lǎo Wáng, Lǎo Wáng juéde zhíwù jiù yīnggāi zìrán shēngzhǎng, liánxù sān zhōu méiyǒu jiāo shuǐ, lǜluó de yèzi quánbù biàn huáng. Xíngzhèng bù de Xiǎo Zhāng kàn bú xiàqù, cóng wǎng shàng chá le yǎnghù fāngfǎ, mǎi le zhuānyòng yíngyǎngyè, hái gěi lǜluó xiū le zhī. Lǜluó zhōngyú huīfù le shēngjī, zhǎng chū le xīn de téngwàn. Xiǎo Zhāng wèi cǐ pō wéi déyì, zài gōngzuò qún lǐ fā le zhàopiàn, biāotí shì 「Xíngzhèng bù zhěngjiù lǜluó」. Jiéguǒ dì èr tiān lǎobǎn lùguò, shuō zhège wèizhì fàng zhíwù bù hé guī, ràng bān dào xiūxi qū. Xiǎo Zhāng bǎ lǜluó bān guòqù zhīhòu, zài yě méiyǒu rén gěi tā jiāo shuǐ le. Yí gè yuè hòu, lǜluó yòu yǎnyǎn-yìxī. Wǒ sīxià juéde zhè pén lǜluó de zāoyù jiǎnzhí shì yí bù wēixíng de zhíchǎng yùyán — měi gè rén dōu juéde tā shì biérén de zérèn, ǒu'ěr yǒu rén chūshǒu xiāngjiù, dàn zhìdù zǒng huì zài zuì bù héshíyí de shíhou chūlái gānyù.
text_en: Our office has a pothos plant, and nobody knows who bought it. It was placed on the windowsill next to the printer, in an extremely awkward position — blocking the path to pick up printouts and interfering with opening the window. But nobody ever took the initiative to move it. The pothos's fate depended entirely on who sat next to it. The previous occupant of that desk, Xiao Li, watered it twice a day, and the roots rotted. Then Lao Wang took over, believing plants should grow naturally, and didn't water it for three consecutive weeks — all the leaves turned yellow. Xiao Zhang from Admin couldn't bear watching anymore, looked up care instructions online, bought special nutrient solution, and even pruned the plant. The pothos finally recovered, growing new vines. Xiao Zhang was quite proud, posting photos in the work group chat titled 'Admin Department Saves Pothos.' The next day the boss walked by and said having a plant in that spot wasn't regulation-compliant, and ordered it moved to the break area. After Xiao Zhang relocated it, nobody watered it again. A month later, the pothos was on its last legs once more. I privately thought the pothos's fate was practically a miniature workplace fable — everyone considers it someone else's responsibility, occasionally someone steps in to save it, but the system always intervenes at the most inopportune moment.

title: The Breakfast Stall Calendar
text_zh: 我家附近的早餐摊有一个不成文的规律，熟客都了然于心。周一是豆浆油条，周二是小笼包，周三是煎饼果子，周四是馄饨，周五是肉夹馍，周末是粥配小菜。摊主老陈从来不贴菜单，也从来不解释为什么这么安排。你问他，他就说：「习惯了。」但如果你仔细观察就会发现，这套安排其实暗含着某种体贴的逻辑。周一大家刚上班，需要热乎乎的东西暖胃，所以是豆浆。周三是一周的中间，最容易疲倦，煎饼果子分量足，能顶到中午。周五快放假了，来个肉夹馍犒劳自己。周末不用赶时间，就慢慢喝粥。有一次我跟老陈聊起这事，他想了想说：「其实最早不是这么排的。最早是我老婆定的，她说吃东西要有节奏，不能天天一样，也不能天天不一样。」他老婆五年前回老家照顾老人了，之后就没再回来。但这套菜单一直沿用至今。我忽然意识到，老陈每天凌晨四点起来准备不同的食材，不只是在做生意，也是在守护一个已经不在身边的人留下的秩序。那些热腾腾的早餐背后，藏着一份被日常掩盖的思念。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā fùjìn de zǎocān tān yǒu yí gè bù chéngwén de guīlǜ, shúkè dōu liǎorán yú xīn. Zhōuyī shì dòujiāng yóutiáo, zhōu'èr shì xiǎolóngbāo, zhōusān shì jiānbing guǒzi, zhōusì shì húntun, zhōuwǔ shì ròujiāmó, zhōumò shì zhōu pèi xiǎo cài. Tānzhǔ Lǎo Chén cónglái bù tiē càidān, yě cónglái bù jiěshì wèishéme zhème ānpái. Nǐ wèn tā, tā jiù shuō: 「Xíguàn le.」 Dàn rúguǒ nǐ zǐxì guānchá jiù huì fāxiàn, zhè tào ānpái qíshí àn hán zhe mǒu zhǒng tǐtiē de luójí. Zhōuyī dàjiā gāng shàngbān, xūyào rè hūhū de dōngxi nuǎn wèi, suǒyǐ shì dòujiāng. Zhōusān shì yì zhōu de zhōngjiān, zuì róngyì píjuàn, jiānbing guǒzi fènliàng zú, néng dǐng dào zhōngwǔ. Zhōuwǔ kuài fàngjià le, lái ge ròujiāmó kàoláo zìjǐ. Zhōumò bú yòng gǎn shíjiān, jiù mànmàn hē zhōu. Yǒu yí cì wǒ gēn Lǎo Chén liáo qǐ zhè shì, tā xiǎng le xiǎng shuō: 「Qíshí zuì zǎo bú shì zhème pái de. Zuì zǎo shì wǒ lǎopo dìng de, tā shuō chī dōngxi yào yǒu jiézòu, bù néng tiāntiān yíyàng, yě bù néng tiāntiān bù yíyàng.」 Tā lǎopo wǔ nián qián huí lǎojiā zhàogù lǎorén le, zhīhòu jiù méi zài huílái. Dàn zhè tào càidān yìzhí yányòng zhìjīn. Wǒ hūrán yìshí dào, Lǎo Chén měitiān língchén sì diǎn qǐlái zhǔnbèi bù tóng de shícái, bù zhǐ shì zài zuò shēngyì, yě shì zài shǒuhù yí gè yǐjīng bú zài shēnbiān de rén liú xià de zhìxù. Nàxiē rèténgteng de zǎocān bèihòu, cáng zhe yí fèn bèi rìcháng yǎngài de sīniàn.
text_en: The breakfast stall near my home follows an unwritten rule that regulars all know by heart. Monday is soy milk and fried dough sticks, Tuesday is soup dumplings, Wednesday is jianbing, Thursday is wontons, Friday is rou jia mo, weekends are congee with side dishes. The stall owner, Lao Chen, never posts a menu and never explains the arrangement. Ask him and he just says, 'Habit.' But if you observe carefully, you'll find this system actually contains a considerate logic. Monday everyone's just starting work, needing something warm to soothe the stomach, hence soy milk. Wednesday is mid-week, when fatigue peaks, and jianbing is filling enough to last until lunch. Friday the weekend is near, so treat yourself to rou jia mo. Weekends there's no rush, so slowly sip congee. Once I chatted with Lao Chen about it, and after thinking he said, 'Actually it wasn't arranged this way at first. My wife set it up originally. She said eating should have rhythm — can't be the same every day, but can't be different every day either.' His wife went back to their hometown five years ago to care for elderly parents, and hasn't returned since. But this menu has continued to this day. I suddenly realized that Lao Chen getting up at four every morning to prepare different ingredients isn't just running a business — it's guarding an order left behind by someone no longer at his side. Behind those steaming breakfasts hides a longing masked by the everyday.

title: The Slow Train
text_zh: 在高铁覆盖了几乎所有干线的今天，我特意选择了一趟绿皮火车，从成都到昆明，全程二十多个小时。车厢里没有WiFi，座椅不能调节角度，空调时有时无。但恰恰因为这些不便，整个旅程有了一种奇特的质感。火车走走停停，经过无数个不在地图上标注的小站。每一站都会上来几个人，带着编织袋或者竹篓，车厢里弥漫着各种混合的气味——方便面、柑橘、烟草、还有泥土。对面坐着一个带孙子的老太太，从一个塑料袋里掏出自家做的酸萝卜，分给周围的人。我接过一块，酸得眯起了眼睛。她笑着说：「我们那边的萝卜就是这个味儿。」窗外的风景不停地变换——先是平原上的油菜花田，然后是连绵的丘陵，接着是峡谷和隧道交替出现。火车钻进隧道的时候，窗户变成了一面黑色的镜子，映出车厢里人们的脸。一个年轻人抱着吉他在打盹，一对情侣头靠着头看同一本书。绿皮火车的时间是慢的、稠的，像一种已经快要被遗忘的生活节奏。到昆明的时候天已经亮了，我走出车站，觉得自己不只是到达了一个目的地，而是穿越了一段正在消失的时间。
text_pinyin: Zài gāotiě fùgài le jīhū suǒyǒu gànxiàn de jīntiān, wǒ tèyì xuǎnzé le yí tàng lǜpí huǒchē, cóng Chéngdū dào Kūnmíng, quánchéng èrshí duō ge xiǎoshí. Chēxiāng lǐ méiyǒu WiFi, zuòyǐ bù néng tiáojié jiǎodù, kōngtiáo shí yǒu shí wú. Dàn qiàqià yīnwèi zhèxiē búbiàn, zhěnggè lǚchéng yǒu le yì zhǒng qítè de zhìgǎn. Huǒchē zǒuzǒu-tíngtíng, jīngguò wúshù gè bú zài dìtú shàng biāozhù de xiǎo zhàn. Měi yí zhàn dōu huì shànglái jǐ gè rén, dài zhe biānzhī dài huòzhě zhúlǒu, chēxiāng lǐ mímàn zhe gè zhǒng hùnhé de qìwèi — fāngbiànmiàn, gānjú, yāncǎo, háiyǒu nítǔ. Duìmiàn zuò zhe yí gè dài sūnzi de lǎo tàitai, cóng yí gè sùliào dài lǐ tāo chū zìjiā zuò de suān luóbo, fēn gěi zhōuwéi de rén. Wǒ jiē guò yí kuài, suān de mī qǐ le yǎnjing. Tā xiào zhe shuō: 「Wǒmen nàbiān de luóbo jiù shì zhège wèir.」 Chuāng wài de fēngjǐng bù tíng de biànhuàn — xiān shì píngyuán shàng de yóucàihuā tián, ránhòu shì liánmián de qiūlíng, jiēzhe shì xiágǔ hé suìdào jiāotì chūxiàn. Huǒchē zuān jìn suìdào de shíhou, chuānghù biàn chéng le yí miàn hēisè de jìngzi, yìng chū chēxiāng lǐ rénmen de liǎn. Yí gè niánqīng rén bào zhe jítā zài dǎdǔn, yí duì qínglǚ tóu kào zhe tóu kàn tóng yì běn shū. Lǜpí huǒchē de shíjiān shì màn de, chóu de, xiàng yì zhǒng yǐjīng kuài yào bèi yíwàng de shēnghuó jiézòu. Dào Kūnmíng de shíhou tiān yǐjīng liàng le, wǒ zǒu chū chēzhàn, juéde zìjǐ bù zhǐ shì dàodá le yí gè mùdìdì, ér shì chuānyuè le yí duàn zhèngzài xiāoshī de shíjiān.
text_en: In an era when high-speed rail covers nearly every main line, I deliberately chose a green-skin train from Chengdu to Kunming, a journey of over twenty hours. The carriage had no WiFi, the seats couldn't recline, and the air conditioning was intermittent. But precisely because of these inconveniences, the whole trip acquired a peculiar texture. The train stopped and started, passing countless small stations unmarked on any map. At each stop a few people would board, carrying woven bags or bamboo baskets, and the carriage filled with mixed smells — instant noodles, tangerines, tobacco, and earth. Across from me sat an old woman with her grandson, who pulled out homemade pickled radish from a plastic bag and shared it with those around her. I took a piece; the sourness made me squint. She laughed and said, 'That's just how radish tastes where we're from.' The scenery outside kept shifting — first rapeseed flower fields on the plains, then rolling hills, then gorges and tunnels alternating. When the train plunged into a tunnel, the window became a black mirror reflecting the faces of people in the carriage. A young man holding a guitar dozed, a couple leaned their heads together reading the same book. Time on the green-skin train is slow, thick, like a rhythm of life that is nearly forgotten. By the time we reached Kunming it was already dawn. Walking out of the station, I felt I hadn't just arrived at a destination, but had passed through a stretch of time that is disappearing.
